coredata.dc:title: A Novel quality-by-design optimized spectrofluorimetric approach for estimation of the oral synthetic adiponectin receptor agonist AdipoRon in different biological matrices: Compliance with greenness metrics
coredata.dc:creator: Magdy, Galal
Elmansi, Heba
Samaha, Mahmoud M.
Said, Eman
El-Enany, Nahed
coredata.dc:description: The current study introduces the first analytical approach for the quantitative analysis of adipoRon, a synthetic adiponectin receptor agonist, using a facile, rapid, sensitive, and green spectrofluorimetric method. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by adopting a fast microwave-assisted procedure using accessible and natural substrates (orange juice and urea). The suggested synthetic method produced CQDs with narrow particle size distribution (2–10 nm), high fluorescence stability, and good quantum yield (29.3%). The produced fluorescent QDs were used as fluorescent sensors and were quantitatively quenched by increasing adipoRon concentrations. A two-level full factorial design (22 FFD) was used to identify the most critical experimental factors affecting the relative fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. The fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs by the drug was measured at 417 nm after excitation at 325 nm. Under the optimized conditions, the quenching of N-CQDs' fluorescent intensity displayed acceptable linearity with the adipoRon concentration range of 5.0–20.0 μg/mL with limits of quantitation and detection of 0.843 μg/mL and 0.278 μg/mL, respectively. Owing to the high sensitivity, the suggested method was efficiently applied for adipoRon estimation in different biological matrices, including spiked human plasma, real rat plasma, and liver homogenate samples with high % recoveries (87.41–103.17%) and low %RSD values (<2.87%). The method's greenness was evaluated using two metric tools, the complementary green analytical procedure index (ComplexGAPI) and the Analytical GREENNESS metric approach (AGREE), and the results showed that the method was excellent green. The proposed methodology was validated according to the ICHQ2 (R1) recommendations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AdipoRon
Quantum dots
Spectrofluorimetry
Greenness

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of nitrogen-doped CQDs
Measuring the quantum yield of the N-doped CQDs
Optimization of the sensing conditions using a univariate approach followed by 22 FFD
Spectral and microscopic characters of N-CQDs
Optimization of different variables using a univariate approach followed by 22 FFD
Real rat plasma and liver homogenate samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Two-level full factorial design (22 FFD) optimization plot.
(A). Fluorescence spectra of N-doped CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (300–380 nm), (B). Fluorescence spectra of N-doped CQDs; (a) excitation and (b) emission spectra.

(A). Fluorescence emission spectra of N-doped CQDs after the addition of different adipoRon concentrations (from top to bottom: 0, 5.0, 8.0, 10.0, 16.0, 20.0 μg/mL), (B). The linearity plot of fluorescence quenching against adipoRon concentration.
Stern-Volmer plots for the quenching of N-doped CQDs fluorescence using different concentrations of adipoRon (5.0, 10.0, 16.0, 20.0 μg/mL) at four different temperatures (298, 303, 313, and 323 K).

(A) 22 FFD Pareto chart of the effects on the RFI at alpha = 0.05, (B) 22 FFD main effects plot for RFI by data means type, (C) 22 FFD normal plot of the effects on the RFI at alpha = 0.05, (D) 22 FFD full interaction plots for RFI by data means type.
Results of greenness assessment of the proposed method using ComplexGAPI (a) and AGREE (b) tools.
Two-level full factorial design (22 FFD) and their dependent response for spectrofluorimetric determination of adipoRon.
Validation data for adipoRon by the proposed spectrofluorimetric method.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: LOQ = 10 Sa/b, where Sa = standard deviation of the intercept and b = slope.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of the proposed method for the determination of adipoRon in pure form.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each reading is the average of three separate determinations.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Application of the proposed method for the determination of adipoRon in spiked human plasma, real rat plasma, and liver homogenate samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Each reading is the average of three separate determination.

head.ce:title: A Novel quality-by-design optimized spectrofluorimetric approach for estimation of the oral synthetic adiponectin receptor agonist AdipoRon in different biological matrices: Compliance with greenness metrics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, New Mansoura University, New Mansoura, 7723730, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, New Mansoura University, New Mansoura 7723730, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The current study introduces the first analytical approach for the quantitative analysis of adipoRon, a synthetic adiponectin receptor agonist, using a facile, rapid, sensitive, and green spectrofluorimetric method. Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) were synthesized by adopting a fast microwave-assisted procedure using accessible and natural substrates (orange juice and urea). The suggested synthetic method produced CQDs with narrow particle size distribution (2–10 nm), high fluorescence stability, and good quantum yield (29.3%). The produced fluorescent QDs were used as fluorescent sensors and were quantitatively quenched by increasing adipoRon concentrations. A two-level full factorial design (22 FFD) was used to identify the most critical experimental factors affecting the relative fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs. The fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs by the drug was measured at 417 nm after excitation at 325 nm. Under the optimized conditions, the quenching of N-CQDs' fluorescent intensity displayed acceptable linearity with the adipoRon concentration range of 5.0–20.0 μg/mL with limits of quantitation and detection of 0.843 μg/mL and 0.278 μg/mL, respectively. Owing to the high sensitivity, the suggested method was efficiently applied for adipoRon estimation in different biological matrices, including spiked human plasma, real rat plasma, and liver homogenate samples with high % recoveries (87.41–103.17%) and low %RSD values (<2.87%). The method's greenness was evaluated using two metric tools, the complementary green analytical procedure index (ComplexGAPI) and the Analytical GREENNESS metric approach (AGREE), and the results showed that the method was excellent green. The proposed methodology was validated according to the ICHQ2 (R1) recommendations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: AdipoRon is a novel oral synthetic adiponectin receptor agonist with a molecular formula of C27H28N2O3. Its chemical name is 2-(4-benzoylphenoxy)-N-(1-benzylpiperidin-4-yl)acetamide (Fig. 1 ) (National Center for Biotechnology Information). It was developed by Okada-Iwabu et al. (2013). Regarding adiponectin, it is one of the various adipokines produced by adipocytes (Hotta et al., 2000) and at high levels from adipose tissues of the lean population (Scherer et al., 1995). It employs favorable antifibrotic, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant effects in the environment of metabolic syndrome (de Jong and Curhan, 2006). Adiponectin stimulates AMP-activated protein kinase, which regulates fatty acid metabolism. Obesity causes a localized inflammatory response in the adipose tissue, which in turn causes alteration in the secretion of adipocyte-derived factors that support pathologies like cancer. Therefore, as adipoRon is an orally active AdipoR1 and AdipoR2 agonist and antidiabetic, the significance of adipoRon is extended to normalize glucose metabolism and prevent obesity. It is orally administered and readily absorbed and delivered to target tissues, so it is potentially clinically relevant.

Only two methods have been described to identify and characterize adipoRon so far. The first method relied on in vitro and in vivo identification and characterization of generated metabolites of adipoRon using liquid chromatography-high resolution/high accuracy (tandem) mass spectrometry using atmospheric-pressure chemical ionization or electrospray ionization techniques (Dib et al., 2016). The second method was devoted to characterizing the adipoRon's metabolites in human and rat liver microsomes by means of UHPLC- Q-Exactive Orbitrap tandem mass spectrometry (Ge et al., 2019).

In recent years, there has been an increasing interest in the uses and applicability of semiconductor nanocrystals called quantum dots (QDs) (Magdy et al., 2023a, 2023b; Alossaimi et al., 2023a). The luminescent carbon QDs have been found attractive in the analytical disciplines for the detection and quantitation of pharmaceutical, biological, and environmental samples utilizing the quenching effect of the analytes on QDs' native fluorescence (Magdy et al., 2021a, 2022a, 2022b, 2023c; Alossaimi et al., 2023b). The importance of these probes is acquired from different points. Firstly, carbon is one of the main elements in biological systems (Zhao et al., 2013). Then, low-cost synthesis approaches have been reported (Li et al., 2012). Additionally, CQDs exhibit distinctive size-controlled chemical and optical properties, bio-compatibility, high chemical stability, and environmentally friendly properties.

From the literature, no methods have yet been reported for the quantitative estimation of adipoRon. Therefore, the current study is devoted to developing a novel spectrofluorimetric approach utilizing quality-by-design (QbD) for the quantitative analysis of adipoRon in its raw materials, spiked human plasma, real rat plasma, and liver homogenate samples without the need for any prior chemical derivatization steps or expensive instrumentation. The current approach depends on the quantitative quenching of the intrinsic fluorescence of naturally synthesized nitrogen-doped CQD. As the studied drug doesn't display native fluorescence characteristics, the significance of the proposed approach is increased. The synthesis of fluorescent nanoprobe based on a microwave-assisted procedure in less than 10 min. The starting materials were orange juice and urea. These N-CQDs showed fluorescence at 417 nm following excitation at 325 nm, and they were found to be stable after synthesis for more than five months.

Quality-by-design (QbD) depends on using the lowest number of experiments to assess the influence of the significant parameters and their interactions on the obtained responses to accomplish the anticipated output (Klein and Rivera, 2000). Experimental design or design of experiments (DOE) aims at creating systematic plans to extract the most information with the fewest experiments possible and to draw significant conclusions from the data with the aid of statistical models (Magdy et al., 2021b, 2023d, 2023e; Hinkelmann, 2012). In the current research, a two-level full factorial design (22 FFD) was chosen as it is a suitable design that permits the screening of many parameters with a minimum number of experiments. In the proposed approach, in order to combine the eco-friendly and sustainability of the synthesis of CQDs, an integrated strategy for greening the synthesis and decreasing the number of experiments for the estimation of the drug is investigated in this work. The proposed strategy relied on the use of green starting materials and low-energy microwave synthesis in addition to using QbD to optimize different measurement parameters.

The greenness profile and eco-friendliness of the proposed technique were examined using two metric tools, including the complementary green analytical procedure index (ComplexGAPI) (Płotka-Wasylka and Wojnowski, 2021) and the Analytical GREENNESS metric approach (AGREE) (Pena-Pereira et al., 2020). These two approaches for assessing greenness confirmed that the proposed method met the greatest number of green requirements, encouraging its usage as a green substitute for routine drug analysis.

AdipoRon pure sample was bought from CSN Pharm (Arlington Heights, USA). The purity is ≥ 98% as certified. Urea, sodium hydroxide, phosphoric, acetic and boric acids, and methanol were obtained from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). All materials and solvents were of analytical grade. Ultrapure water was used during the whole experiments.
Samples of pooled human plasma were provided by the blood bank of Kafrelsheikh University Hospitals (Kafrelsheikh, Egypt) and were kept frozen at −80 °C until use following gentle thawing.

Twenty-four male Swiss Albino mice (weighing 25 ± 5 g) were utilized. Mice were housed in plastic cages at a temperature of 25 °C, given access to free food and water, and kept on a 12-h light/dark cycle. The experimental procedure was carried out in the animal house, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University.

Mice were given adipoRon with two doses by gavage for 3 consecutive days. They were anesthetized by thiopental (70 mg/kg, i.p.) for euthanasia. All mice had blood samples taken from the retro-orbital plexus, and the sera were separated by centrifuging the samples for 15 min at 4000 rpm, which were stored at − 80 °C for further analysis. The liver was harvested from all the mice. A part of the left lobe was homogenized in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (10% w/v) and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant was kept at − 80 °C for biochemical measurements.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Agilent Technologies' Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer with a Xenon flash lamp was utilized to obtain the emission fluorescence spectra (Santa Clara, CA 95051, United States).

The Sigma 2-16P benchtop cooling centrifuge (Germany), VM-300P vortex mixer from Gemmy Industrial Corp (Taiwan), domestic microwave (GE614ST, 2800 W, 2450 MHz, Samsung, Malaysia), ultrasonic bath (SS 101H, USA), a Jenway pH-meter (3510, UK) and membrane filters (0.45 μm, Phenomenex, USA) were also used.
UV–Visible double beam spectrophotometer (PG Instruments Ltd, UK).

32 scans at a resolution of 4 cm−1 were conducted using Thermo-Fisher Scientific Nicolet - iS10 FT-IR Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA) with a Ge/KBr beam splitter and a 4000 to 1000 cm−1 DTGS detector.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and Energy Dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) were carried out using the JEM-2100 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) by JEOL (Tokyo, Japan) operated at 200 Kv.
Minitab® Software (Release 16, State College, Pennsylvania) was used to conduct a statistical evaluation of the FFD.

ce:section.ce:para: A standard solution of adipoRon was created at a concentration of 100.0 μg/mL in methanol and diluted with distilled water as appropriate.

Britton-Robinson buffer was prepared with a concentration of 0.02 M by adjusting the pH of the aqueous solution containing 0.04 M of each of boric acid, orthophosphoric acid, and acetic acid with 0.2 M NaOH to get a pH range from 2 to 12.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of nitrogen-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Three grams of urea were dissolved in 50 mL of orange juice and heated in a microwave for 10 min. The product was cooled and diluted to 100 mL with double distilled water. Aggregated particles were eliminated by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 15 min. A filtration process was performed to get a clear solution that was diluted to 200 mL again with double distilled water. From this QDs stock solution, 10.0 mL were transferred into a 100.0 mL measuring flask and completed to the mark with water to prepare the working solution used in the spectrofluorimetric approach.
After preparation, the QDs were characterized using variable spectroscopic and microscopic methods, including spectrophotometry, spectrofluorimetry, FT-IR, and HRTEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measuring the quantum yield of the N-doped CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The following equation (1) was applied to measure the quantum yield (QY) of the prepared N-doped CQDs (Simões et al., 2017; Rurack, 2008):
(1)
Φ
N − C Q D s
=
Φ
Q S
×
(
F
N − C Q D s
/
F
Q S
)
×
(
A
Q S
/
A
N − C Q D s
)
×
(
η
N − C Q D s
/
η
Q S
)
2

Φ: QY, F: the integrated emission intensity, η: solvent refractive index, and A: absorbance. 0.1 M H2SO4 was used to prepare Quinine sulfate (QS), which was the standard with 0.54 QY at 350 nm. The ratio ηN-CQDs/ηst equals 1 in an aqueous solution.

A series of 5.0-mL measuring flasks were prepared, and 1.0 mL 0.02 M Britton-Robinson buffer (pH 4) was added. Then, 125.0 μL of N-CQDs were transferred to each flask. Aliquots of adipoRon solution were added to obtain the final concentration in the range of 5.0–20.0 μg/mL. The flasks were completed with double distilled water up to the mark. The fluorescence response was recorded at excitation wavelength = 325 and emission wavelength = 417 nm. The calibration plot and regression equation were created by graphing the fluorescence quenching values vs. the concentration (μg/mL).

Aliquots from the human plasma (1.0 mL) and 125.0 μL of N-CQDs were transferred into Falcon tubes (15 mL) series and spiked with increasing concentrations of adipoRon to get concentrations in the range (5.0–8.0 μg/mL). Vortex mixing was applied for 2 min; then, the tubes were completed with methanol to 5.0 mL to precipitate the plasma protein. The tubes were then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min to separate the drug from the plasma components. Then, 1.0 mL of the supernatant was transferred into a set of 5.0-mL measuring flasks after filtration using syringe filters (0.45 μm) to ensure the complete removal of any residual endogenous plasma components after protein precipitation and centrifugation. Then, the same procedures mentioned under section ''2.4.4.'' were followed with a blank experiment conducted similarly. The values of fluorescence quenching were graphed vs. the concentration (μg/mL) to construct the calibration plot and, consequently, the regression equation.

The procedures mentioned under section ''2.4.5.'' were followed to construct the calibration curves for biological rat samples using 1.0 mL aliquots of blank rat plasma or rat liver homogenate. The concentration of the real rat plasma or liver homogenate samples was computed using the corresponding regression equations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the sensing conditions using a univariate approach followed by 22 FFD

ce:section.ce:para: AdipoRon is a small-molecule adiponectin mimetic. It is the first synthetic analog of endogenous adiponectin that binds to the adiponectin receptors AdipoR1 and AdipoR2 and induces insulin sensitivity, inhibits lipid synthesis, and increases glucose metabolism. Based on this importance, in this study, a novel spectrofluorimetric method was developed for the sensitive estimation of adipoRon by the quenching of nitrogen-doped CQDs' native fluorescence without any pre-derivatization steps or sophisticated techniques. The significance of the current study is expanded due to its applicability in different biological matrices. Tunable photoluminescent N-CQDs were prepared through a facile microwave-assisted technique employing the pyrolysis of orange juice and urea as precursors. As was reported previously, passivation of carbon dots via nitrogen-doping improves their photoluminescent characters (Manioudakis et al., 2019), therefore we prepared N-CQDs and utilized these properties to design the proposed method, where all the experimental factors were optimized using an FFD model.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spectral and microscopic characters of N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized N-doped CQDs optical properties were revealed by UV-absorption spectroscopy. It was found that they displayed two distinctive absorption peaks at 275 and 213 nm, corresponding to the n-π*/π-π* transitions (Kim et al., 2020; Li et al., 2015), as illustrated in Fig. S1. In addition, strong blue fluorescence under UV light and dark orange color under visible light with a lengthy homogenous phase was observed (Fig. S1). The QDs' emission exhibited excitation dependency across the 300–380 nm range, where the optimal fluorescence emission was measured after excitation at 325 nm, as demonstrated in Fig. 3 A. The optimal mirror image excitation and emission wavelengths of the N-doped CQDs solution were located at 325 and 417 nm, respectively (Fig. 3B). We further considered the fluorescence stability of the synthesized QDs, and it was found to be more than 5 months with a high QY of 29.3%. HR-TEM micrographs presented in Fig. S2 indicated the spherical shapes and homogenous distribution of QDs with a distribution particle size in the range of 2–10 nm. Also, FT-IR was utilized to investigate the N-CQDs' surface function groups, and the spectrum showed the following peaks: C–H (595 cm−1), CC (1658 cm−1), CO (1701 cm−1), C–N (2097 cm−1), and O–H/N–H (3500-3100 cm−1) (Fig. S3). To ascertain their composition and validate the level of nitrogen doping, the prepared N-CQDs were also subjected to an elemental analysis using EDX (Magdy et al., 2023f). The results show that a high nitrogen doping level was achieved because the N-CQDs primarily consisted of carbon (59.21%), oxygen (24.70%), and nitrogen (16.08%) (Fig. S4).

By increasing adipoRon concentration, the fluorescence of the prepared N-CQDs was decreased quantitatively, as displayed in Fig. 4 . According to the interaction between quantum dots and adipoRon as a quencher, the potential quenching mechanism was investigated using the Stern-Volmer equation (2) (Żamojć et al., 2021; Magdy et al., 2022c, 2023g):
(2) F0/F = 1 + Ksv[Q] = 1 + Kqτ0[Q]
Where: F is the drug-QD fluorescence intensity, F0 is for QDs alone, [Q] is the drug's molar concentration, KSV denotes the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, kq represents the quenching rate constant, and τ0 is the fluorophore's average lifetime (10−8 s).

KSV values at four temperatures (298, 303, 313, 323 K) were calculated after the fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) was plotted against four different concentrations of AdipoRon [Q] (Fig. 5 ). At 298, 303, 313, and 323 K, respectively, Ksv values were found to equal 5.47 × 103, 4.71 × 103, 3.73 × 103, and 3.16 × 103 L mol −1. Based on these values, the quenching may be attributed to the static quenching mechanism, where the Ksv values decreased by increasing the temperature (Zu et al., 2017). The values of kq were subsequently computed and found to equal 5.47 × 1011, 4.71 × 1011, 3.73 × 1011, and 3.16 × 1011 L mol−1.s−1, respectively. The kq values were significantly greater than the maximum diffusion rate constant (2.0 × 1010 L mol−1.s−1), which further confirmed the static quenching mechanism (Lakowicz, 2006). In addition, since there was no overlapping between the excitation spectra of N-CQDs and the UV–vis absorption spectra of adipoRon, the inner filter effect was excluded as a quenching mechanism (Fig. S5).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of different variables using a univariate approach followed by 22 FFD
ce:section.ce:para: The experimental factors that could influence the fluorescence quenching were examined to achieve a high sensitivity for the suggested method. This was done by initially optimizing the variables using a preliminary univariate optimization strategy, followed by 22 FFD as follows:

The buffer pH had a substantial impact on the fluorescence quenching of QDs. Consequently, various experimental trials were performed by modifying the pH of the medium (ranging from 2 to 12) using Britton-Robinson buffer. The fluorescence quenching of each experiment was monitored in order to select the suitable pH domain. The maximum fluorescence quenching occurred within the pH range of 4–6. When the pH was adjusted outside this range, the fluorescence quenching was significantly reduced (Fig. S6a). Thus, a pH range of 4–6 was chosen as the designated buffer pH domain in the FFD.

The fluorescence quenching of QDs was significantly affected by the volume of the buffer. The influence of buffer volume was investigated by using the Britton-Robinson buffer solution in quantities ranging from 0.5 to 3 mL. An optimal response in terms of quenching the fluorescence emission of QDs was noticed with a buffer volume of 1–2 mL. However, when the volume was adjusted either lower or higher than this range, the fluorescence quenching decreased (Fig. S6b). Therefore, a range of 1–2 mL was chosen as the domain for buffer volume in the FFD.

The impact of different incubation times on the fluorescence quenching of QDs by AdipoRon was examined at various time intervals ranging from 1 to 60 min. The observation revealed that the quenching of QDs occurred immediately and remained constant for a minimum of 60 min (Fig. S6c). As a result, the incubation time was not considered a significant factor affecting the dependent response (RFI) in the FFD.

As revealed from the initial studies, buffer volume and pH were the two independent variables that had the greatest effect on the dependent response (RFI). Following the identification of each factor's domain as described above, four planned experiments were used to carry out 22 FFD, as shown in Table 1. The four experiments' results were then entered into the software, where the response optimizer was used to maximize the desired response (Table S1). To evaluate optimization, the composite desirability (D), which has a range of 0–1, was used. A large composite desirability score was established in the present study, indicating that the conditions are satisfactory. The software recognized the ideal conditions, which were determined to be pH 4 and a buffer volume of 1.0 mL, using the optimization plot (Fig. 2) and the desirability value.

The ability to identify and arrange the most pertinent affecting variables on dependent response is one of the most important advantages of using QbDs. Additionally, it makes it possible to study the interactions between these variables, which is impossible using conventional optimization methods (Weissman and Anderson, 2015). Several plots produced by the Minitab software, such as the Pareto Chart, main effects plot, normal plot of the effects, and full interaction plot, can be used to accomplish this (Fig. 6 ). Additionally, based on the estimated coefficients (data in coded units) of the independent variables, as displayed in Table S2, it offers a mathematical analysis of RFI response. According to the Pareto chart, main effects plot, and normal plot of the effects, pH has the largest non-significant impact on the RFI. According to the values of the estimated effects, this parameter has a negative impact on RFI, which means that as pH rises, RFI falls. Additionally, the RFI is negatively impacted by buffer volume. In contrast, RFI was negatively impacted by the interaction between buffer volume and pH the least, as indicated by the lowest values in the estimated effects (Table S2).
The proposed approach has been verified and validated according to ICH recommendations (ICH, 2005). Different validation criteria were considered to assure the validity of the developed methodology.

For the construction of the calibration plot, the values of fluorescence quenching (ΔF) were graphed vs. the final concentrations of adipoRon in μg/mL. In the proposed method, concentrations from 5.0 to 20.0 μg/mL showed acceptable linearity, as presented in Fig. 4. Analytical values such as correlation coefficient (r), intercept, and slope were summarized in Table 2 .

LOQ and LOD have been computed in consonance with ICH recommendation (ICH, 2005) by means of mathematical equations. LOD was calculated to be 0.278 μg/mL, while LOQ was 0.843 μg/mL (Table 2), indicating the high sensitivity of the suggested method, which permitted the analysis of adipoRon in different biological matrices.

The high accuracy of the suggested method was also verified by the high % recoveries obtained (99.64–100.71%), as presented in Table 3 . In addition, the %RSD values were found to be below 2.0%, revealing the acceptable inter-day and intra-day precisions of the suggested spectrofluorimetric approach (Table S3).

The method's ability to detect the studied drug unaffectedly in the presence of different metal ions, including Ba+2, Ca+2, Mg+2, K+, and Na+ (Fig. S7A), demonstrated its selectivity. To rule out any potential for interference and to confirm the method's high selectivity, the effects of lactose, maltose, mannitol, citric acid, and dextrin as common excipients were thoroughly examined (Fig. S7B). They almost had no impact on the N-CQDs' fluorescence intensity. In addition, the ability to determine adipoRon in intricate biological matrices supported the proposed method's selectivity. The method produced low %RSD values and reasonably high recovery percentages without experiencing much interference from the plasma components (Table 4 ). As a result, the developed method showed excellent selectivity for the estimation of adipoRon without any interference.

Owing to the high sensitivity, the suggested method was utilized to assay adipoRon in spiked human plasma samples. Plasma levels of adipoRon differ from one report to another (Hotta et al., 2000; Akimoto et al., 2018), but they are all almost covered by the proposed new methodology. The regression equation (y = 43.6x - 211.9) was constructed in plasma after spiking with different concentrations of the cited drug (Table 4). Good %recoveries were obtained (97.06–103.17%), which proved that the suggested method could be applied efficiently to determine the drug in human plasma. Accordingly, the developed method could hold potential applications for the therapeutic drug monitoring of the studied drug.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real rat plasma and liver homogenate samples
ce:section.ce:para: The developed method was also applied for the determination of the studied drug in real rat plasma and liver homogenate samples. The results illustrated in Table 4 revealed the appropriateness of the method for the assay of adipoRon in rat plasma and liver homogenate samples with satisfactory %recoveries and %RSD values.

More and more people throughout the world are realizing how important it is to manage waste and dangers in order to improve green analysis. The study employed two green metric tools, namely the Analytical GREENNESS metric approach (AGREE) (Pena-Pereira et al., 2020) and the complementary green analytical procedure index (ComplexGAPI) (Płotka-Wasylka and Wojnowski, 2021), to determine the greenness profile of the suggested method. By including an extra hexagonal field at the bottom of GAPI (Płotka-Wasylka, 2018), ComplexGAPI is a unique greenness assessment tool. To cover the entire range of parameters that define the analytical technique, ComplexGAPI is necessary. It describes the steps taken prior to sample preparation and final analysis, including workup, purification, chemicals and solvents, equipment, environmental factors, yield, and the connection to the green economy. Each field in the hexagon-shaped ComplexGAPI pictogram represents a distinct phase in the given process. If the hexagon is green, the related criteria have been met. The suggested method's greenness was effectively estimated using the ComplexGAPI (Fig. 7 a). The advantages of a simple sample pre-treatment (protein precipitation) are offered by the suggested approach. Due to the micro-scale extraction, there is a high degree of sensitivity. The technique seems to be environmentally beneficial because it uses mild conditions and produces very little waste from chemicals and reagents. In the process of creating the N-CQDs, orange juice was utilized as a green carbon source. Furthermore, the energy consumption of the spectrofluorimeter is minimal. With regard to AGREE, it is a tool that assesses twelve significant criteria in order to identify the environmental and occupational dangers that are part of the analytical technique. The ultimate result falls between 0 and 1.0. Excellent green methodology is demonstrated by the way the strategy produced good outcomes and a high score, as seen in Fig. 7b.

AdipoRon has many favorable characters as a novel therapeutic agent. It is used in the management of metabolic-associated diseases, such as type 2 diabetic-nephropathy. It is also a protective agent in case of cardiac hypertrophy, postischemic myocardial apoptosis, and secondary brain injury. These beneficial effects in clinical conditions necessitate the development of novel analysis methods for its therapeutic drug monitoring. Herein, we report a green, economic, and sensitive spectrofluorimetric method for adipoRon determination. The proposed method depends on naturally microwave-assisted synthesized nitrogen-doped CQDs as a more eco-friendly choice than metal-based fluorescent probes. The starting materials were orange juice and urea as carbon and nitrogen sources, respectively. These QDs showed fluorescence at 325/417 nm, and adipoRon exerts a quenching effect on the QDs fluorescence band. The proposed approach was developed, optimized, and validated for the quantitative analysis of adipoRon in its pure form and different biological samples. The method was validated following ICH guidelines for implementation in QC laboratories.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

All experiments were performed in accordance with relevant guidelines and regulations and this work was approved by the Committee of Research Ethics in the Faculty of Pharmacy, Kafrelsheikh University, Kafrelsheikh, Egypt.

Galal Magdy: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology, Validation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Heba Elmansi: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Mahmoud M. Samaha: Investigation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Eman Said: Data curation, Investigation, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Nahed El-Enany: Conceptualization, Investigation, Resources, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
